data.title: Regionals, smaller banks expected to benefit more than G-SIBs in proposed capital rules, analysts say
data.description:
U.S. banking regulators' new proposals for banks' capital requirements will lower the common equity tier 1 requirements for most banks, which could free them up to lend more or to increase capital returns to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.

J.P. Morgan analyst Vivek Juneja calls the new proposals "a more holistic view of capital requirements" as they factor in stress test changes. They also reduce double counting in some areas, such as operational risk and market risk, he added.

"Regulators hope this would shift some lending activity that had moved to non-banks over the past 10 years back to banks, with one asking what more could or should be done to provide relief to banks so that it could benefit economic activity," Juneja wrote in a note to clients.

Bank equity analysts estimate that, if finalized, the new rules would lower CET1 by ~4.8% at the biggest banks, by ~5.2% for large regional banks, and by 7.8% for smaller banks.

Goldman Sachs, including additional costs resulting from the rules, estimates ~3.8% reduction for category 1 and 2 banks.

Most of the reduction for the  biggest U.S. banks, classified as category 1 and 2, will come from a recalibration of the Global Systemically Important Bank (G-SIB) surcharge. The improvement for category 3 and 4 banks will be largely from revised risk weighting, Citi analyst Benjamin Gerlinger said.

Note that category 1 banks are G-SIBs, category 2 banks have consolidated assets of $700B or more, category 3 banks have total consolidated assets of between $250B and $700B, and category 4 banks have at least $100B of consolidated assets but less than $250B.

Positives for banks include lower risk weights for residential mortgages and elimination of the deduction for mortgage servicing rights, Gerlinger added. Risk-weightings of 90% on retail loans under the Standardized Approach was a surprise to the downside, he said.

A  key nuance, though, is retail loans qualify for a 75% risk weight under the ERBA  (Expanded Risk-Based Approach), and credit card transactors receive a 45% risk weight, he noted.

Citi's initial analysis sees JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Goldman Sachs (GS), and Bank of America (BAC) benefiting the most from the G-SIB surcharge recalibration.

The change in residential mortgage risk weightings, expected to fall to ~35% from 50%, should benefit Citizens Financial Group (CFG), Citi said. Eliminating the MSR deduction from CET1 should benefit Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB), Regions Financial (RF), and Truist Financial (TFC), which were near threshold levels, Gerlinger said.

Evercore ISI analyst John Pancari expects Goldman Sachs (BS), Morgan Stanley (MS), and BNY (BK) to benefit most from the G-SIB reduction. The best-positioned category 3 and 4 banks include Citizens Financial Group (CFG), Huntington Bancorp (HBAN), and M&T Bank (MTB), he added. 

"We acknowledge that this positioning may not necessarily mean a step-up in capital return is in order (particularly against the uncertain macro backdrop and rating agency considerations) but helps identify those with added capital optionality," Pancari wrote in a note to clients.

Goldman Sachs analyst Ryan M. Nash sees the proposal as constructive for regional banks and card issuers given lower risk weights across corporate and consumer assets, a longer-than-expected phase-in timeline for updated "accumulated other comprehensive income," and no change to unfunded commitment capital charges for category 3 and 4 banks.

"All together, we think the capital changes should be well-received and provides an opportunity for the industry to accelerate loan growth or buybacks, particularly given the AOCI phase-in timing," Goldman's Nash wrote.

The KBW Nasdaq Bank Index (BKX) rose 0.4%, and the KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index slipped 0.3% in midday Friday trading. The G-SIBs are trading in the green: Citigroup (C) +0.6%, JPMorgan Chase (JPM)  +0.1%, Morgan Stanley (MS) +1.9%, Goldman Sachs (GS) +1.0%, Bank of America (BAC) +0.4%, and Wells Fargo (WFC) +1.3%.

Large regionals were mostly up, with a couple of slight dips: U.S. Bancorp (USB) -0.4%, Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) +0.7%,  Truist Financial (TFC) +0.1%, Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) -0.1%, Citizens Financial Group (CFG) +0.2%, PNC Financial Services (PNC) -0.1%, Regions Financial (RF) flat, and KeyCorp (KEY) +0.5%.

Consumer finance stocks were mixed: Capital One Financial (COF) -0.1%, American Express (AXP) -0.1%, Synchrony Financial (SYF) +0.7%, and Bead Financial (BFH) +0.8%.

data.title: The Iran conflict puts Six Flags' Saudi Arabia vision on a roller coaster
data.description:
Six Flags' (FUN) Qiddiya City theme park in Saudi Arabia has not been directly damaged by the Iran conflict, but the broad threat to the Middle East threatens to dampen the property's performance by disrupting regional air travel, depressing inbound tourism, and raising perceived risk for foreign visitors.

Six Flags Qiddiya City opened at the end of 2025 as part of the Qiddiya giga‑project near Riyadh and is being operated under a management and licensing agreement by Six Flags (FUN), while ownership and capital risk sit with Qiddiya Investment Company, backed by the Public Investment Fund. While the impact on Six Flags (FUN) earnings will be minimal due to the asset-light model, analysts think extended Middle East tourism concerns could limit further expansion in the region.

The current U.S.–Israel conflict with Iran has triggered airspace closures and flight disruptions across parts of the Gulf, with episodes of airspace shutdowns and even temporary embassy closures or damage complicating departures for tourists transiting regional hubs. Analysts now expect a meaningful drop in inbound arrivals to the wider Middle East, with one tourism estimate suggesting a decline of more than 20% in visitor numbers this year to the region, depending on the duration of the war, which directly threatens the growth assumptions behind Saudi Arabia’s leisure mega‑projects, including Qiddiya.

data.title: Strategy initiated with Buy rating at Texas Capital
data.description:
Texas Capital Securities initiated coverage of Strategy (MSTR) with a Buy rating and $200 price target, citing a compelling entry point for the digital asset leader.

According to analyst Randy Binner, Strategy is the clear leader in digital asset treasury management. The shares have seen volatility around Bitcoin prices, but the management remains dedicated to building BTC per share over time.

"We regard BTC as an observable Level 1 asset with transparent quarterly mark-to-market accounting," said Binner in a research note.

"The quality and innovation of management, diversity of digital credit offerings, execution around BTC per share, and Mosaic pivot in the software business all argue for franchise value over 1.19x NAV in our opinion, which could set up further potential price target upside," said the research note.

MSTR shares closed at $138.24 on Thursday.

Texas Capital's rating differs from the average Seeking Alpha authors and Quant rating of Hold. The Wall Street community grades MSTR as Strong Buy.

data.title: CBS News to cut 6% of workforce; shutters New Radio division -- report
data.description:
As CBS’s new editor-in-chief, Bari Weiss is leading sweeping changes at CBS News with support from Paramount Skydance (PSKY) CEO David Ellison.

In a memo to employees viewed by CNN, Weiss and CBS News president Tom Cibrowski said some parts of the newsroom “must get smaller to make room for the things we must build to remain competitive.” While Weiss and Cibrowski did not mention the number of employees who will be let go in their memo, the number is reportedly around 6%, representing roughly 60 to 70 employees among a staff of 1,100.

The division is also shuttering its century-old News Radio effective May 22.

“Unfortunately, [the decision to end the news radio service] means that all positions within the CBS News Radio team are being eliminated…as the shift in radio programming strategies, coupled with challenging economic realities, has made it impossible to continue the service.”

Weiss, along with Cibrowski, has been tasked with transforming the legacy news channel into a digital-first, balanced network through staff cuts, an ideological shift, and attracting younger audiences by pivoting towards a more digital presentation.

After leaving The New York Times amid editorial disagreements, Weiss founded Substack Common Sense, later morphing into a media company renamed The Free Press that was  “built on the ideals that were once the bedrock of American journalism.” After Paramount Skydance (PSKY) bought The Free Press for $150M in October 2025, Paramount CEO David Ellison tapped Weiss for the top job at CBS News. 

While the job cuts are likely part of a broader shift within the news division, artificial intelligence has also resulted in significant workforce reductions across editorial and production functions. In 2025, roughly 17,000 media and entertainment jobs were eliminated, representing ~18% of the industry, with most—though not all—of those cuts attributed to the adoption of artificial intelligence.

data.title: U.S. stocks' decline persists despite efforts to minimize concerns about the Iran war, oil prices sustain
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were in the red, with oil sustaining higher prices even as Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu said the country is helping to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.7%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.5%.

“Remarkable with everything that's going on that the S&P 500 ((SP500)) is only down 6% from its high. Market seems to be pricing in a short-lived war, with crude oil crashing back down. This is the hopeful outcome, but is the market always right? No. Far from it,” said Charlie Bilello, chief market strategist at Creative Planning.

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields spiked as markets reassessed the near-term impact of monetary policy after the Federal Reserve announced its decision to keep interest rates unchanged.

The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 11 basis points higher at 4.36%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 8 basis points to 3.88%.

Oil prices slipped late on Thursday on the news that Benjamin Netanyahu said Iran no longer has the capacity to enrich uranium or make ballistic missiles.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly lower at $97.2 on Friday, while Brent futures (CO1:COM) were at $109.8 per barrel.

In addition, the U.S. military is reportedly sending thousands more Marines to the Middle East, including three warships, according to U.S. officials on Friday.

“Oil at $150 or $200 isn't the no-brainer it's made out to be. You have to think carefully about what's priced at $110. My best guess is that current levels price half of all oil out of the Gulf being permanently disrupted,” said Robin Brooks, senior fellow at The Brookings Institution.

There are no major economic reports slated for the day.

data.title: Diversified Royalty reports Q4 results

data.title: SolarEdge hits 52-week high as Jefferies upgrades on Europe energy volatility
data.description:
SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG) surges 12.8% in Friday's trading to a 52-week high of $53.28 as Jefferies upgraded the solar power company to Hold from Underperform with a $49 price target, raised from $30, saying the escalating Middle East war is triggering a replay of the European energy security dynamics that turbocharged the company's business during the Russia-Ukraine war.

The invasion of Ukraine boosted SolarEdge's (SEDG) European revenues to a $1.9B peak in 2023 from $630M in 2020; benchmark TTF gas has surged 94% to more than €60/MWh since the Iran war began, and Jefferies analyst Julien Dumoulin-Smith said he now sees a clear catalyst for renewed solar demand acceleration in Europe, where geopolitical energy shocks drive households and businesses to seek energy independence through distributed solar.

Solar and renewable penetration has increased dramatically across the major markets in Europe since the Russia-Ukraine conflict—as evidenced by the relatively stable power pricing despite surging TTF—so the coming level of surge may not equal that seen during 2020-23, but the outlook on SolarEdge's (SEDG) earnings profile "has definitely improved from here, even though the extent of it remains unclear," the analyst wrote.

However, with SolarEdge (SEDG) shares now trading near recent peaks due to the Middle East conflict, Dumoulin-Smith thinks the current valuation adequately reflects the potential demand surge, justifying a Hold rating.

data.title: Apple CEO says latest product launch produces best week ever for new Mac customers
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) CEO Tim Cook said the hardware launch initiated earlier this month produced the best week ever in attracting first-time Mac buyers.

The surge in sales was likely prompted by the release of the new, low-cost MacBook Neo, which starts at $599.

"Mac just had its best launch week ever for first-time Mac customers," Cook said in a post on X. "We love seeing the enthusiasm!"

In addition to the Neo, Apple also released a new MacBook Air, MacBook Pro, and two new Studio Displays. All these were made available on March 11.

While the Neo is available at multiple Apple Store locations, a delivery order made today from the Apple site indicates an 18-to-25-day delivery time. In contrast, the new MacBook Pro and MacBook Air indicate a delivery time of only four days.

The MacBook Neo received rave reviews after its release. Bloomberg said it was a "game changer" for the Cupertino-based company, while Wired called it "a new kind of Mac for a new generation." CNET said it offers the same premium design and durability but is much more affordable than Apple's traditional MacBooks. Forbes said they would be "shocked" if the Neo is not the best-selling MacBook model in 2026. They also expect it to take market share from Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) Chromebooks and Microsoft (MSFT) Windows 11 laptops. Forbes also noted that the Neo is 50% faster for everyday tasks than Intel (INTC) Core Ultra 5 PCs.

Apple is expected to ship 4.5M to 5M MacBook Neos this year, according to TF International Securities analyst Ming-Chi Kuo. Moreover, Apple's supply chain dominance allows it to maintain the low price on Neo, while other laptop models might start to show price increases due to higher memory costs.

data.title: AstraZeneca, Sanofi expand R&D footprint in China
data.description:
AstraZeneca (AZN) and Sanofi (SNY) have become the latest drugmakers in the West to expand R&D operations in China, a market with highly sought-after R&D prospects for the global pharmaceutical industry.

Anglo-Swedish drugmaker AstraZeneca (AZN) took to its Chinese social media account on Thursday to announce it would build an innovation Center ‌in Shanghai, China’s commercial capital.

The company said it will also establish a cell therapy ​manufacturing and supply base in Shanghai. The facility will manufacture and supply CAR‑T cell therapies for China and other ​Asian markets, making the U.K.-based firm the first global drugmaker with end-to-end cell therapy facilities in the country.

Additionally, citing the state-run Securities Times on Thursday, Reuters reported that AZN plans to build a production and supply base for radioconjugate drugs ⁠in the southern city of Guangzhou as part of an initiative to provide markets in China and the Asia-Pacific with cancer drugs.

On Thursday, Astra’s European rival Sanofi (SNY) ​also launched an ‌innovation and operation center in ​the southwestern ​Chinese city of ⁠Chengdu.

The French drugmaker ​said on its Chinese ​social media account on Friday that it expects to utilize the facility to support its R&D ​and ​clinical ⁠operations, as well as manufacturing and ​supply chain ​services in China.

Western pharmaceutical companies have increasingly turned to China to strike drug licensing deals. According to a recent analysis by Goldman Sachs, Chinese biopharma companies have accounted for 46% of new drug molecules that entered into human studies in the first half of 2025.
